findings.title: Secret detected: Detected a Generic API Key, potentially exposing access to various services and sensitive operations.
findings.description: Betterleaks detected a Detected a Generic API Key, potentially exposing access to various services and sensitive operations. in battle_test_candidates/go_target/main.go:31. Rule: generic-api-key. Entropy: 4.92
findings.tool_output: Rule: generic-api-key | Tags: [] | Entropy: 4.92 | Verified: false

findings.title: Potential G704: SSRF via taint analysis
findings.description:
SSRF via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends HTTP request to endpoint that calls http.Get(targetURL) at line 157 with a user-controlled URL (e.g., http://internal-service/admin). 2. The server makes a request to that URL, potentially accessing internal resources.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can probe internal network services, access cloud metadata endpoints (e.g., 169.254.169.254), or exploit internal APIs, leading to information disclosure or lateral movement.
attack_scenario.explanation: The code directly uses user input as a URL without validation or allowlist, enabling SSRF.

findings.title: Potential G701: SQL injection via taint analysis
findings.description:
SQL injection via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
71 | 70: 	query := "SELECT id, username, role FROM users WHERE username='" + username + "' AND password='" + password + "'"
71: 	row := db.QueryRow(query)
72:

attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a login request with username and password parameters. 2. These are concatenated directly into SQL query at line 70. 3. Attacker can inject SQL commands (e.g., ' OR '1'='1) to bypass authentication or extract data.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can bypass authentication, read, modify, or delete arbitrary database records, potentially gaining admin access or exfiltrating sensitive data.
attack_scenario.explanation: String concatenation of user input into SQL query without parameterization leads to SQL injection.

findings.title: Potential G703: Path traversal via taint analysis
findings.description:
Path traversal via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends request with filename parameter containing path traversal sequences (e.g., ../../etc/passwd). 2. At line 135, filename is concatenated with './files/' and passed to os.ReadFile at line 136. 3. Server reads arbitrary files outside intended directory.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can read sensitive files (e.g., /etc/passwd, application source code, credentials) leading to information disclosure and potential privilege escalation.
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is used in file path construction without sanitization, enabling path traversal.

findings.title: Potential G702: Command injection via taint analysis
findings.description:
Command injection via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[Also reported by another step: Subprocess launched with variable
Gosec confidence: 90%]

attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends request with cmd parameter containing shell metacharacters (e.g., ; rm -rf /). 2. At line 114, cmd is passed to exec.Command("bash", "-c", cmd). 3. The shell executes arbitrary commands.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary system commands, leading to full server compromise, data exfiltration, or lateral movement.
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is passed directly to a shell command without sanitization, enabling command injection.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends request with input parameter containing JavaScript payload (e.g., <script>alert(1)</script>). 2. At line 210, the input is embedded in JSON response without escaping. 3. If the response is rendered in a browser (e.g., via script tag), the payload executes.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in victim's browser, leading to session hijacking, data theft, or phishing.
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is reflected in HTTP response without proper encoding, enabling XSS despite Content-Type header.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — Content-Type is application/octet-stream, which forces download. No XSS vector exists because browsers will not render the response as HTML.]

attack_scenario.explanation: Content-Type is application/octet-stream, which forces download. No XSS vector exists because browsers will not render the response as HTML.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — Content-Type is text/plain, which prevents script execution. Browsers will not interpret the response as HTML, so XSS is not possible.]

attack_scenario.explanation: Content-Type is text/plain, which prevents script execution. Browsers will not interpret the response as HTML, so XSS is not possible.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends request with input parameter containing HTML/JavaScript (e.g., <script>alert(1)</script>). 2. At line 100, the input is written directly to response without escaping. 3. Browser renders the malicious HTML, executing the script.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in victim's browser, leading to session hijacking, data theft, or phishing.
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is written directly to response with Content-Type text/html (implied by w.Write([]byte(html))), enabling stored/reflected XSS.

findings.title: Potential G401: Use of weak cryptographic primitive
findings.description:
Use of weak cryptographic primitive
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
206 | 205: 	// VULNERABLE: MD5 is cryptographically broken
206: 	hash := md5.Sum([]byte(input))
207: 	hexHash := fmt.Sprintf("%x", hash)

attack_scenario.actor: attacker with ability to obtain hash values (e.g., via data breach)
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker obtains MD5 hash from the application (e.g., via API response). 2. Attacker uses precomputed rainbow tables or brute force to reverse the hash due to MD5's weaknesses.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can recover the original input (e.g., password) from the hash, leading to credential compromise.
attack_scenario.explanation: MD5 is cryptographically broken and should not be used for security-sensitive hashing.

findings.title: Potential G114: Use of net/http serve function that has no support for setting timeouts
findings.description:
Use of net/http serve function that has no support for setting timeouts
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker initiates many slow connections to the server. 2. Because no timeouts are set, connections remain open indefinitely, exhausting server resources.
attack_scenario.impact: Denial of service (DoS) due to resource exhaustion, making the application unavailable.
attack_scenario.explanation: http.ListenAndServe without timeouts is vulnerable to slowloris and similar DoS attacks.

findings.title: Potential G202: SQL string concatenation
findings.description:
SQL string concatenation
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
70 | 69: 	// VULNERABLE: string concatenation into SQL query
70: 	query := "SELECT id, username, role FROM users WHERE username='" + username + "' AND password='" + password + "'"
71: 	row := db.QueryRow(query)

attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends login request with malicious username/password. 2. At line 70, input is concatenated into SQL query. 3. Attacker can inject SQL commands.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can bypass authentication, read/modify database, leading to data breach or privilege escalation.
attack_scenario.explanation: String concatenation in SQL query enables SQL injection.

findings.title: Potential G304: Potential file inclusion via variable
findings.description:
Potential file inclusion via variable
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends request with filename containing path traversal. 2. At line 135-136, file is read. 3. Attacker reads arbitrary files.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can read sensitive files, leading to information disclosure.
attack_scenario.explanation: User input used in file path without sanitization enables path traversal.

findings.title: Potential G107: Potential HTTP request made with variable url
findings.description:
Potential HTTP request made with variable url
Gosec confidence: 60%

attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends request with targetURL parameter. 2. At line 157, http.Get is called with that URL. 3. Server makes request to attacker-controlled or internal URL.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can perform SSRF, accessing internal services or cloud metadata.
attack_scenario.explanation: User-controlled URL passed to http.Get without validation enables SSRF.

attack_scenario.actor: attacker with ability to obtain hash values
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker obtains MD5 hash from application. 2. Attacker reverses hash using rainbow tables or brute force.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can recover original input, leading to credential compromise.
attack_scenario.explanation: Importing crypto/md5 indicates use of weak cryptographic primitive.

findings.title: Potential Generic API key in config
findings.description: Regex pattern detected a potential Generic API key in config at main.go:31. This may be a hardcoded secret that automated scanners missed.
findings.code_snippet: 31 | const apiKey = "sk-abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345pqr678stu"

attack_scenario.actor: Any attacker with access to the source code repository (e.g., via a public repo, insider threat, or compromised CI/CD pipeline). No special privileges required beyond read access to the codebase.
attack_scenario.path:
1. Attacker obtains the source code (e.g., clones public repo, accesses leaked internal repo).
2. Attacker reads line 31 of main.go: `const apiKey = "sk-abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345pqr678stu"`.
3. Attacker extracts the API key string.
4. Attacker uses the API key to authenticate to the corresponding service (e.g., OpenAI, Stripe, etc.) and perform unauthorized actions.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker gains full access to the API service associated with the key, potentially leading to data exfiltration, service abuse, financial charges, or privilege escalation depending on the service's capabilities.
attack_scenario.explanation: The finding is a hardcoded API key in source code. The key pattern 'sk-...' strongly suggests a secret key for a service like OpenAI or Stripe. Hardcoded secrets are a well-known vulnerability; the attacker only needs read access to the code to exploit it. The impact is direct and severe.

findings.title: SBOM generated: 1 components detected
findings.description: Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) generated in CycloneDX format. 1 components inventoried for vulnerability tracking.

findings.title: Not a git repository — supply chain checks limited
findings.description: Supply chain security checks (SBOM provenance, SLSA) require a git repository. Without git history, we cannot verify commit signatures or build provenance.

findings.title: SBOM generation available via syft
findings.description: Run 'syft scan <target> -o cyclonedx-json' to generate a Software Bill of Materials. SBOMs are required for supply chain transparency (US Executive Order 14028).
findings.fix_suggestion: syft scan battle_test_candidates/go_target -o cyclonedx-json > sbom.cdx.json

findings.title: OpenSSF Scorecard not installed
findings.description: Install OpenSSF Scorecard (https://github.com/ossf/scorecard) to get automated security health checks for your dependencies and build pipeline.
findings.fix_suggestion: go install github.com/ossf/scorecard/v5@latest

findings.title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) in handleProxy
findings.description: The handleProxy function takes a user-supplied URL via the 'url' query parameter and makes an HTTP GET request to that URL without any validation or allowlist. An attacker can make the server send requests to internal services (e.g., 169.254.169.254 for cloud metadata, localhost, internal APIs) or external systems, potentially leading to information disclosure, port scanning, or further attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement an allowlist of permitted domains/IPs, validate the URL against a strict regex, or use a URL parser to ensure only safe schemes (e.g., https) and hosts are allowed. Consider using a dedicated proxy library with built-in restrictions.

findings.title: OS Command Injection in handleExec
findings.description: The 'cmd' parameter from the URL query string is passed directly to exec.Command with bash -c, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary system commands. No input validation or sanitization is performed.
findings.fix_suggestion: Avoid using shell commands with user input. If necessary, validate the input against a whitelist of allowed commands, or use a safer alternative like exec.Command with separate arguments instead of bash -c.

findings.title: Path Traversal in handleFiles
findings.description: The 'filename' parameter from the URL query is concatenated into a file path without validation, allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files outside the intended './files/' directory by using path traversal sequences like '../'.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate the filename to ensure it does not contain path traversal characters (e.g., '../' or absolute paths). Use filepath.Clean and check that the resolved path starts with the intended base directory. Alternatively, use a whitelist of allowed filenames.

findings.title: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) in handleSearch
findings.description: User input from the 'query' query parameter is directly concatenated into an HTML string and written to the response without any escaping or sanitization. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript by crafting a malicious query parameter, leading to XSS.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use Go's html/template package to auto-escape output, or manually escape the query value with html.EscapeString() before embedding it in HTML.

findings.title: Missing authentication on proxy endpoint
findings.description: handleProxy allows anyone to make arbitrary HTTP requests without any authentication. This can be used for SSRF attacks, internal network scanning, and data exfiltration.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add authentication middleware (e.g., session token, API key) to restrict access to authorized users only.

findings.title: Missing input validation on URL parameter
findings.description: The 'url' parameter is taken directly from user input and used in an HTTP request without any validation, sanitization, or allowlist. This enables SSRF attacks against internal services.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate the URL against an allowlist of permitted domains/IPs, or restrict to a specific set of safe URLs. Consider using a URL parser to validate scheme and host.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on proxy endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting is applied to this endpoint, allowing an attacker to make unlimited requests, potentially for SSRF scanning or DDoS amplification.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting per IP or per user to restrict the number of requests within a time window.

findings.title: Missing authentication on command execution endpoint
findings.description: handleExec executes arbitrary shell commands without any authentication check. This allows unauthenticated remote code execution.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add authentication middleware (e.g., session token, API key, or JWT validation) before executing any commands. Consider removing this endpoint entirely or restricting to admin-only access.

findings.title: Missing input validation on command parameter
findings.description: The 'cmd' parameter is passed directly to exec.Command without any validation, sanitization, or allowlisting. This enables arbitrary command injection.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate and sanitize the 'cmd' parameter. Use an allowlist of permitted commands and arguments. Avoid passing user input to shell interpreters like bash -c.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on command execution endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting is applied to this endpoint, allowing an attacker to execute unlimited commands or perform brute-force attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting (e.g., token bucket, per-IP throttling) to restrict the number of requests per time period.

findings.title: Missing authentication on file access endpoint
findings.description: handleFiles allows reading arbitrary files from the server without any authentication. An attacker can enumerate and read any file in the ./files/ directory.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add authentication middleware or check session/JWT token before processing the request.

findings.title: Missing input validation on filename parameter
findings.description: The filename parameter is used directly in a file path without any validation. This allows path traversal attacks (e.g., '../../etc/passwd') to read arbitrary files outside the intended directory.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate the filename: reject paths containing '..' or '/', use filepath.Base() to sanitize, or use a whitelist of allowed filenames.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on file access endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting is applied to this endpoint, allowing an attacker to rapidly enumerate files or perform denial-of-service attacks by requesting many files.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting middleware (e.g., token bucket or sliding window) to restrict requests per IP or user.

findings.title: Missing authentication on search endpoint
findings.description: handleSearch does not perform any authentication check. While search may be a public feature, the lack of auth combined with reflected XSS vulnerability increases risk. Authentication should be considered to restrict access and enable audit logging.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add authentication middleware or session check at the start of the function. For example: if !isAuthenticated(r) { http.Error(w, "Unauthorized", http.StatusUnauthorized); return }

findings.title: Missing input validation on search query
findings.description: The 'query' parameter is taken directly from URL query string and used in HTML output without any validation, sanitization, or escaping. This allows reflected XSS attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate and sanitize the query parameter. Use html.EscapeString() or a proper template engine that auto-escapes. Also consider limiting query length and rejecting dangerous characters.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on search endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting is applied to the search endpoint. This could allow automated abuse, such as scraping or DoS attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting per IP or per user. Use a middleware like tollbooth or implement token bucket algorithm.

findings.title: Missing authentication on hash endpoint
findings.description: handleHash performs a cryptographic operation without any authentication check. While not accessing user data, it exposes a hash function that could be abused for hash computation or as an oracle.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add authentication middleware or require an API key to access this endpoint.

findings.title: Missing input validation on hash input
findings.description: The 'input' query parameter is not validated for length or content. An attacker could provide extremely long input causing resource exhaustion or inject special characters that break the JSON response.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate input length (e.g., max 1024 characters) and sanitize for JSON output. Use json.Marshal instead of string formatting.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on hash endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting is applied to this endpoint. An attacker could abuse it for hash computation, potentially leading to resource exhaustion or using it as a hash oracle for password cracking.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting (e.g., per IP or per user) to restrict the number of requests per time window.

findings.title: Missing input validation on username and password
findings.description: The handler directly uses user-supplied username and password in a SQL query without any validation or sanitization, leading to SQL injection vulnerability.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate username and password for expected format, length, and character set. Use parameterized queries or prepared statements to prevent SQL injection.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on login endpoint
findings.description: The login endpoint has no rate limiting, allowing brute-force attacks on user credentials.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting per IP or per username. Consider using a middleware or a dedicated rate limiter library.

findings.title: Missing CSRF protection on login endpoint
findings.description: The login endpoint accepts POST requests without CSRF protection, making it vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement CSRF tokens for state-changing requests. For API endpoints, consider using SameSite cookies or custom headers.

findings.title: Missing Content-Type validation
findings.description: The handler does not validate the Content-Type header of incoming requests, potentially allowing unexpected content types to be processed.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate that the Content-Type header is 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' or 'multipart/form-data' for form submissions. Reject requests with unexpected Content-Type.

findings.title: Missing authentication on admin user list endpoint
findings.description: handleAdminUsers returns all user data without any authentication check. Anyone can access this endpoint and enumerate all users.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add auth middleware. Example: wrap handler with RequireAuth() or check session token at function start.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on admin user list endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting is applied to this endpoint, which could allow an attacker to enumerate users or perform brute-force attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting middleware or use a library like 'golang.org/x/time/rate' to limit requests per IP or user.
